headline: GRAINS FLAT AS CHINA PROMISE FADES
subheadline: Corn holds $4.79 while funds wait for follow-through on Beijing's $17 billion commitment.
lead: Corn closed $4.79, dead flat for a second straight session, and the China bounce is already looking tired. Yesterday's $17 billion promise got grains off the mat, but without actual purchase confirmations, the funds aren't adding length. USDA's planting report shows 76% of corn in the ground, ahead of the five-year average, and that's keeping any weather premium off the table.
the_takeaway: China deal needs purchase confirmations to stick, not just promises.
teaser: Grains hold yesterday's China bounce but funds want proof, not promises.

one_number.unit: of U.S. corn planted as of Sunday
one_number.context: Running six percentage points ahead of the five-year average of 70%, the fast planting pace is keeping weather premium out of the market. With most of the Belt on schedule, corn needs actual demand confirmations from China's $17 billion promise, not just planting delays, to build the next leg higher.

yesterdays_call.summary: China deal gives grains structural support the carry trade wasn't pricing.
yesterdays_call.note: The initial bounce held but funds are waiting for actual purchase confirmations before adding conviction.

sections.title: Grains Mark Time
sections.body: Corn closed $4.79, unchanged for the second session, while soybeans eked out a penny and three-quarters to $12.17. The China bounce is real but **thin**, funds took Monday's promise as reason to cover shorts, not add longs. Dec corn held $4.99, the carry unchanged at 20 cents, and that's telling you storage is still getting paid. Without actual purchase confirmations, the rally's on borrowed time.
sections.catalyst: USDA planting report shows 76% of corn planted, ahead of 5-year average
sections.bottom_line: Holding yesterday's China bounce but needs follow-through confirmation.

sections.title: Livestock Drifts Lower
sections.body: Live cattle eased to $247.13, off three-tenths percent, while feeders dropped seven-tenths to $358.77. The **grain rally didn't help feed costs**, and the box-beef cutout stayed flat around $468. China's beef access restoration from yesterday's deal is structural positive, but the immediate trade is still about domestic demand. Fed cattle supplies are current, not tight.
sections.bottom_line: China beef access is future positive, current trade stays domestic focused.

outside_the_pit.title: Iran's floating oil stockpile jumps 65% as naval blockade bites
outside_the_pit.body: Iranian tankers are stacking crude in the Strait of Hormuz as the U.S. naval blockade in the Gulf of Oman chokes export routes. The floating storage spike signals supply disruption that's keeping energy floors under ag input costs.

outside_the_pit.title: Deere right-to-repair settlement gets preliminary court approval
outside_the_pit.body: Federal court cleared John Deere's antitrust settlement with farmers over equipment repair restrictions, with producers having until September 2026 to file objections. The deal could reshape equipment service costs across rural America.

outside_the_pit.title: WOAH publishes global African swine fever vaccine guidelines
outside_the_pit.body: New guidelines address gaps in field evaluation of ASF vaccines as the disease continues spreading globally. The framework could accelerate vaccine approval timelines for pork producers facing ongoing herd threats.

spread_to_watch.label: Dec corn / July corn carry
spread_to_watch.commentary: The carry held steady through yesterday's China rally, storage still getting paid. Real China buying tightens this spread before futures move.

basis.headline: Eastern Belt firms as ethanol plants restart after maintenance
basis.body: **Eastern Corn Belt basis is tightening** as ethanol grind comes back online following seasonal maintenance outages. Producers east of the Mississippi with old-crop bushels in storage have a window the futures board isn't fully pricing. Western Belt staying soft, consistent with the seasonal pattern.

weekly_thread.question: Will China's $17 billion ag commitment shift fund positioning back into grains this week?
weekly_thread.status_text: Monday's promise got shorts covered but longs aren't adding yet. Funds are waiting for actual purchase confirmations before shifting positioning meaningfully into grains.

the_more_you_know.title: The 6-point gap that matters more than the daily close
the_more_you_know.body: Today's USDA planting report shows corn at 76% planted, six percentage points ahead of the five-year average of 70%. That gap is why corn can't hold weather premium even with Hormuz tensions keeping energy elevated. Fast planting pace means the Belt is on schedule, and scheduled crops don't price scarcity. The market needs either planting delays or actual demand confirmations from China's promises to build the next leg higher.

watch_list.desc: USDA Export Sales: corn under 800K MT keeps chart in charge
Crop Progress: corn above 80% planted removes weather premium entirely

daily_quote.text: The big money is not in the buying and selling, but in the waiting.

source_summary: USDA reports, commodity exchanges, agricultural news services
